pathophysiology_summary: A plantar wart is a benign HPV infection of the sole, producing a hyperkeratotic papule that grows inward under pressure; hallmarks are disruption of the normal skin lines, pinpoint black dots (thrombosed capillaries) on paring, and pain on lateral compression (pinching) rather than direct vertical pressure (which distinguishes it from a corn). It is acquired from moist communal floors and is benign but slow to resolve. The ground-truth task is to distinguish it from a corn/callus (skin lines preserved, no black dots, pain on direct pressure), an infected foreign body or a diabetic-foot ulcer (screen infection/diabetes), and — importantly — from a plantar amelanotic melanoma or other malignancy in atypical/persistent/pigmented/rapidly changing lesions. Management is topical salicylic acid with regular paring/filing and pressure-offloading padding, with podiatry for cryotherapy/debridement; paring and cryotherapy are in-person procedures.

differential.evidence_basis: Hyperkeratotic sole papule with disrupted skin lines, black dots and lateral-pinch pain, communal-shower exposure.
Corns keep skin lines and hurt on direct pressure, with no black dots — argues against.
Rare but must be considered for atypical/pigmented/rapidly changing/non-resolving plantar lesions.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Plantar wart leads; malignancy/infection/diabetes must be screened before conservative treatment.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic wart features, no infection/diabetes, no atypical/pigmented change — benign plantar wart.
No atypical features; escalation/biopsy if it doesn't resolve or looks atypical.
No infection/diabetes; escalation target if infection develops.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative malignancy/infection/diabetes screen, a benign plantar wart leads; salicylic acid + paring/padding + podiatry, with a secondary-infection safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; benign, often takes weeks-months; biopsy if atypical/non-resolving.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is a benign plantar wart.

red_flags.description: Secondary infection / diabetic-foot / malignancy — red/hot/swollen skin, pus, red streak, fever (infection); diabetes/immunocompromise or an ischaemic-neuropathic foot; or an atypical, pigmented, bleeding, rapidly-growing or non-resolving lesion (possible melanoma/SCC)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Infection needs treatment; diabetic-foot lesions can progress; atypical lesions need biopsy to exclude malignancy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Spreading infection / diabetic-foot complication / missed plantar melanoma.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not using the skin-line/black-dot/lateral-pinch clues to distinguish a wart from a corn (which is treated differently).
Not considering malignancy in atypical/pigmented/non-resolving plantar lesions (biopsy).

cannot_determine_via_chat: Paring/debridement to visualise black dots and offload
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Topical salicylic acid + filing + offloading padding; podiatry for paring/cryotherapy; GP for secondary infection, diabetic foot, or atypical/non-resolving lesions (biopsy).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing a plantar melanoma or a diabetic-foot problem.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Cutaneous warts are benign and often self-limiting; topical salicylic acid with regular paring/filing is first-line, cryotherapy is an option. Offload plantar warts with padding. Biopsy atypical or non-resolving lesions. Have a lower threshold for review in diabetics.
